1.title: Crossing the Jordan
1.text: After Moses' death, Joshua becomes Israel's leader and is told to cross the Jordan River to take possession of the promised land of Canaan.

2.title: The scarlet line
2.text: The red cord in Rahab's window marked the house to be spared when Jericho fell. For her faith she was saved with her family and, according to the New Testament, became an ancestor of King David.

2.question: What report did the two spies bring back to Joshua?
options: The Lord had delivered the land and its people fainted with fear
The city was too strong to take
The king of Jericho had fled

By showing a pillar of fire over the camp
By building an altar of stones in the wilderness
By dividing the land among the twelve tribes

3.title: A heap of water near Adam
3.text: The text places the damming of the Jordan far upstream, by the city Adam beside Zaretan. The waters flowing down toward the salt sea (the Dead Sea) were cut off, opening a crossing right against Jericho.

4.question: How many men does the Lord tell Joshua to take, one from each tribe?

4.title: A sign for the children
4.text: The twelve stones were meant to prompt a question: when children asked "What mean ye by these stones?", parents would retell how the Lord cut off the Jordan before the ark of the covenant.

4.template: these stones shall be for a {0} unto the children of Israel for ever

and encamped in {0}, in the east border of Jericho

5.title: The kings' hearts melted
5.text: When the Amorite and Canaanite kings heard the Lord had dried up the Jordan, their hearts melted and no spirit remained in them. Fear of Israel cleared the way for conquest before any battle began.

5.template: Make thee sharp {0}, and circumcise again the children of Israel the second time.

5.question: Why did Joshua need to circumcise the people?
options: The generation born in the wilderness had not yet been circumcised
The Egyptians had forbidden them to do it
It was a punishment for disobeying Moses
To heal a sickness in the camp

5.template: Wherefore the name of the place is called {0} unto this day.

5.question: What happened to the manna after Israel ate of the fruit of the land?
options: It ceased and they had manna no more
It turned bitter and no one ate it
It kept falling all that year

5.template: Loose thy shoe from off thy foot; for the place whereon thou standest is {0}.

6.title: Trumpets of rams' horns
6.text: The seven trumpets were made of rams' horns (the shofar), an instrument Israel used to gather the people and announce sacred moments, not as a weapon of war.

6.template: the seventh day ye shall compass the city seven times

6.question: As the people compassed the city, what did the priests do continually?

only Rahab the harlot shall live

7.title: One man's sin, the nation's guilt
7.text: Although only Achan took of the accursed thing, the verse says 'the children of Israel committed a trespass.' Israel was seen as a covenant whole, so one individual's failure affected the entire nation before God.

7.template: they chased them from before the gate even unto {0}

7.question: According to the Lord in verse 11, what had Israel done wrong?
options: Transgressed the covenant, taken of the accursed thing, stolen and lied
Worshipped foreign idols in the camp
Refused to cross the river Jordan
Failed to offer sacrifices at the ark

a goodly {0} garment, and two hundred shekels of silver
the name of that place was called, The valley of {0}

7.title: The valley of Achor
7.text: The name 'Achor' is linked to the idea of trouble, echoing Joshua's question to Achan: 'Why hast thou troubled us?' The great heap of stones raised there stood as a memorial of the event 'unto this day.'

8.title: What changed from Jericho
8.text: At Jericho everything was accursed, but at Ai the Lord allowed Israel to keep the spoil and cattle as a prey for themselves, even though the city and its people were destroyed.

8.template: and Joshua chose out {0} mighty men of valour, and sent them away by night.
and they went to lie in ambush, and abode between {0} and Ai, on the west side of Ai:

9.title: An elaborate disguise
9.text: The Gibeonites staged a full performance: torn sacks and wine bottles, patched shoes, and dry, mouldy bread, all to look as if they had traveled from far away and escape Israel's ban on making a league with neighboring peoples.

9.question: What reason did the ambassadors give Joshua for coming from so far?
options: Because of the name of the Lord his God and his fame
To buy grain during a famine
To bring tribute to the king of Egypt
To seek refuge from a war among the Amorites

9.template: And the men took of their victuals, and asked not {0} at the mouth of the Lord.
Let them live; but let them be hewers of wood and drawers of {0} unto all the congregation;

9.question: What lasting service did Joshua impose on the Gibeonites for their deception?
options: To be hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of God
To pay an annual tribute of silver
To serve as soldiers in Israel's army
To leave the land and never return

10.template: the five kings of the {0}, the king of Jerusalem

Wait one more day before you attack

Make peace with the five kings

10.title: The day the sun stood still
10.text: This chapter records the famous miracle of the sun standing still for about a whole day, and it cites the ancient book of Jasher, a lost work mentioned only once more in the Bible (2 Samuel).

11.title: Jabin of Hazor
11.text: Hazor was the greatest of the northern Canaanite city-states, described here as the head of all those kingdoms. Its king Jabin summons the other kings to resist Israel.

11.template: and to the Hivite under {0} in the land of Mizpeh.
And the Lord said unto {0}, Be not afraid because of them

12.title: From Arnon to Hermon
12.text: The chapter frames the conquered territory between the river Arnon to the south and mount Hermon to the north, mapping in a single stroke the whole strip taken east of the Jordan.

12.template: {0} king of the Amorites, who dwelt in Heshbon

13.title: Unfinished work
13.text: Even after many victories, the Lord tells Joshua that 'very much land' still remained to be possessed. The conquest of Canaan was gradual, and not all the territory was occupied at once.

13.template: Thou art old and stricken in years, and there remaineth yet very much {0} to be possessed.

Because they had already received land beyond Jordan

Because they were the smallest tribe

14.title: Forty-five years remembered
14.text: Caleb recalls being forty when Moses sent him to spy out the land; now eighty-five, he says his strength for war is the same, after forty-five years of waiting on God's promise.

14.template: gave unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh Hebron for an inheritance

15.title: The first lot
15.text: Judah was the first tribe to receive its inheritance west of the Jordan. As descendants of Jacob's fourth son, from Judah would come the royal line of David and, later, the Savior himself.

16.title: Why Joseph becomes two tribes
16.text: Because the tribe of Levi received no territory (it was set apart for priestly service), Joseph fills two of the twelve allotments through Manasseh and Ephraim, keeping the count of inheritances intact even with no tribe of Levi on the map.

17.title: The daughters of Zelophehad
17.text: The five daughters of Zelophehad — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah — had earlier appealed to Moses (Numbers 27) and, with no male heirs, won the right to inherit land, a landmark case for female inheritance in Israelite law.

18.title: Shiloh, Israel's center
18.text: Shiloh housed the tabernacle and served as Israel's chief sanctuary for generations, down to the days of Eli and Samuel, long before Jerusalem rose to prominence.

19.title: Simeon inside Judah
19.text: Simeon was the only tribe whose inheritance lay entirely within another tribe's land: its cities sat within Judah's inheritance, peculiarly fulfilling the prophecy that Simeon would be "scattered in Israel."

19.question: Which tribe received the second lot, with cities such as Beer-sheba and Moladah?

20.title: Refuge from the avenger of blood
20.text: The cities of refuge shielded the unintentional killer from the "avenger of blood," the kinsman charged with avenging a death. They restrained immediate revenge, ensuring a trial before any punishment.

20.question: Why would the city not deliver the slayer to the avenger of blood?
options: Because he smote his neighbour unwittingly and hated him not beforetime
Because he had paid a ransom of silver
Because he was one of the elders of the city
Because the high priest had pardoned him

21.template: The Lord commanded by the hand of {0} to give us cities to dwell in

21.title: Levites without a homeland
21.text: Unlike the other tribes, the Levites received no continuous territory. Instead they were given cities scattered throughout Israel, so that priestly service and the teaching of the Law could reach every tribe.

22.title: The tribes beyond Jordan
22.text: Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh had been granted land east of the Jordan in Moses' day, but agreed to cross the river and fight until the other tribes won Canaan before returning home.

23.title: The successor of Moses
23.text: Joshua led Israel into the Promised Land after Moses died; this chapter records one of his final addresses, given when he was 'old and stricken in age.'

24.title: Abraham's idol-serving fathers
24.text: Verse 2 reveals that Terah, the father of Abraham, and Israel's ancestors "served other gods" before God's call, a reminder that the nation's faith began with God setting Abraham apart from an idol-worshipping family.
